ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes a distinct, new perspective—Direct Future Prediction (DFP)—that reframes control as supervised prediction of future measurement vectors conditioned on sensory input, action, and a parameterized goal, which is different from standard scalar-reward RL approaches. It clearly differentiates itself by showing advantages over strong baselines (DQN, A3C, DSR), demonstrating goal flexibility at test time, and achieving empirical validation (winning the Visual Doom AI Competition). These elements constitute a non-trivial and notable novelty rather than a purely incremental combination of existing techniques. | B(4): The paper proposes a notably different perspective (Direct Future Prediction) that reframes control as supervised regression of future measurement vectors conditioned on sensory input, actions, and a goal, rather than standard value- or policy-based RL. Its use of vectorial, temporally-dense multi-horizon predictions and explicit goal-conditioning that allows test-time goal changes represents a non-trivial methodological departure from prior work. The strong empirical gains over DQN/A3C/DSR and the reported competition win further indicate the idea is a meaningful and informative innovation, though it is not a radical theoretical breakthrough.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a distinct, new perspective—Direct Future Prediction (DFP)—that reframes control as supervised prediction of future measurement vectors conditioned on sensory input, action, and a parameterized goal, which is different from standard scalar-reward RL approaches. These elements constitute a non-trivial and notable novelty rather than a purely incremental combination of existing techniques.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a notably different perspective (Direct Future Prediction) that reframes control as supervised regression of future measurement vectors conditioned on sensory input, actions, and a goal, rather than standard value- or policy-based RL. Its use of vectorial, temporally-dense multi-horizon predictions and explicit goal-conditioning that allows test-time goal changes represents a non-trivial methodological departure from prior work. The strong empirical gains over DQN/A3C/DSR and the reported competition win further indicate the idea is a meaningful and informative innovation, though it is not a radical theoretical breakthrough.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper is very well organized with a clear progression from problem statement to method to experiments and conclusion, and its main contributions (Direct Future Prediction and its benefits) are stated early. Notation is introduced and used consistently (e.g., F, f, o/s, m, g, θ) when describing the supervised prediction setup. Experimental presentation is informative—datasets, baselines (DQN, A3C, DSR), and evaluation metrics (health, frags, training speed) are clearly reported—and the writing quality is described as excellent with thorough related work coverage, yielding a very readable manuscript. | B(5): The paper is very well organized with a clear progression from method (DFP) to experiments across multiple ViZDoom scenarios and a concise conclusion. The main contributions are stated early and the method notation (e.g., prediction function F, goal-weighted argmax selection) is presented clearly and consistently. Experimental presentation is effective — datasets, baselines, metrics, and hyperparameter reporting are explicit — and the overall writing quality is described as excellent with thorough related-work coverage, yielding a highly readable paper.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is very well organized with a clear progression from problem statement to method to experiments and conclusion, and its main contributions (Direct Future Prediction and its benefits) are stated early. Notation is introduced and used consistently (e.g., F, f, o/s, m, g, θ) when describing the supervised prediction setup. Experimental presentation is informative—datasets, baselines (DQN, A3C, DSR), and evaluation metrics (health, frags, training speed) are clearly reported—and the writing quality is described as excellent with thorough related work coverage, yielding a very readable manuscript.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is very well organized with a clear progression from method (DFP) to experiments across multiple ViZDoom scenarios and a concise conclusion. The main contributions are stated early and the method notation (e.g., prediction function F, goal-weighted argmax selection) is presented clearly and consistently. Experimental presentation is effective — datasets, baselines, metrics, and hyperparameter reporting are explicit — and the overall writing quality is described as excellent with thorough related-work coverage, yielding a highly readable paper.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are clearly stated and appear empirically supported: the paper evaluates DFP against appropriate baselines (DQN, A3C, DSR) on multiple ViZDoom scenarios, reports meaningful metrics (health, frags, training speed), and cites a competition win. The methodology (predicting future measurement vectors per action and selecting actions by g^T F) is coherent and training by regression on collected experience is technically sound. Minor concerns remain about reporting of statistical significance/variance and reproducibility (no code mentioned), but these do not undermine the experimental conclusions presented. Overall the technical evidence largely supports the claims with only small methodological reporting gaps. | B(4): The main claims are clearly stated and appear to be supported by empirical evidence: DFP is evaluated against appropriate baselines (DQN, A3C, DSR) across multiple ViZDoom scenarios, uses meaningful metrics (health, frags, training speed), and even reports winning a public competition. There are no apparent mathematical errors in the described supervised-prediction formulation and action-selection rule, and the experimental methodology includes relevant baselines and reported hyperparameters. However, the summary does not mention statistical significance, multiple random seeds, or code release for reproducibility, which are notable omissions though they do not obviously undermine the reported results. Overall the technical claims are well supported with minor concerns about experimental rigor and reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and appear empirically supported: the paper evaluates DFP against appropriate baselines (DQN, A3C, DSR) on multiple ViZDoom scenarios, reports meaningful metrics (health, frags, training speed), and cites a competition win. The methodology (predicting future measurement vectors per action and selecting actions by g^T F) is coherent and training by regression on collected experience is technically sound. Minor concerns remain about reporting of statistical significance/variance and reproducibility (no code mentioned), but these do not undermine the experimental conclusions presented. Overall the technical evidence largely supports the claims with only small methodological reporting gaps.

There are no apparent mathematical errors in the described supervised-prediction formulation and action-selection rule, and the experimental methodology includes relevant baselines and reported hyperparameters. However, the summary does not mention statistical significance, multiple random seeds, or code release for reproducibility, which are notable omissions though they do not obviously undermine the reported results. Overall the technical claims are well supported with minor concerns about experimental rigor and reproducibility.

main_claims: Introduces Direct Future Prediction (DFP), a supervised-learning approach that predicts future measurement vectors conditioned on current sensory input, measurements, action, and a parameterized goal to choose actions.
Shows that DFP outperforms state-of-the-art deep RL methods (DQN, A3C, DSR) on multiple ViZDoom/Doom-based 3D scenarios, with especially large gains on complex tasks with richer measurements.

Demonstrates that training with vectorial, temporally dense measurements and multi-horizon prediction improves performance compared to scalar rewards, and that models trained without a fixed goal can adapt to new goals at test time.
Reports that a model trained with the presented approach won the Full Deathmatch track of the Visual Doom AI Competition, outperforming the second-best submission by more than 50%.
_summary.methodology: Train a deep network F to predict future differences of selected measurement vectors f for each possible action given current observation (image s and measurements m) and a goal vector g, then select actions by argmax_a g^T F(o_t,a,g;θ); training uses regression on collected experience (DFP).
datasets: ViZDoom (Doom) scenarios: D1 Basic, D2 Navigation, D3 Battle, D4 Battle 2, D3-tx, D4-tx, D4-tx-L
metrics: Average health at end of episode
Average frags (kills) at end of episode
Training speed (millions of simulation steps per day)
limitations: Model is purely reactive (no explicit memory or recurrent components) as presented.
No temporal abstraction or hierarchical skill organization in the presented model.
Designed and evaluated for discrete action spaces; applying to continuous actions is left for future work.
Predicting full sensory input (instead of designated measurements) is stated as beyond current capabilities.
_summary.topic_area: Reinforcement learning / sensorimotor control — supervised prediction for visual navigation and combat in 3D environments
